prompt: Choose one historical period (e.g., Ancient Greece, Islamic Golden Age, Scientific Revolution) and write a 500-word essay on how mathematics influenced science, society, or culture during that time.
intro_note: Period-controlled rewrite focused on the twentieth-century computer revolution. Compresses ancient history into one background clause, names mechanisms (formal logic, binary, encoding), anchors each body on a named in-period figure (Shannon, Turing, von Neumann, ENIAC), and devotes a full paragraph to the science / society / culture impact the prompt asks for. Hover any highlighted phrase for the band-7 craft note.

spans.text: Building on numerical traditions reaching back to Babylonian base-60

highlight.label: Background participle clause
highlight.explanation: "Building on…" front-loads pre-twentieth-century history into ONE subordinate clause so the rest of the essay can stay inside the chosen period. The original spends half of Body 1 outside the computer revolution — this single move fixes period drift.

spans.text: , the twentieth-century computer revolution
rested on three mathematical foundations

highlight.label: Thesis names a mechanism
highlight.explanation: Replaces "In this essay, I will discuss…" (a topic announcement) with a real claim. "Rested on three mathematical foundations" both states a position AND tells the marker what the bodies will deliver.

spans.text: formal logic, binary representation and the numerical encoding of all information

highlight.label: Three-mechanism roadmap
highlight.explanation: Previews EXACTLY three concrete mathematical mechanisms — the Band 5→6 gating fix the feedback flags. Body 1 develops mechanism 2 (binary + logic), Body 2 develops mechanism 3 (encoding); the colon list also matches mechanism 1 (formal logic) implicitly.

spans.text: . This essay examines how these mathematical foundations
transformed computation from an abstract theory into a technology that reshaped scientific research, communication and everyday economic life

highlight.label: Scope-of-impact clause
highlight.explanation: The prompt asks how mathematics influenced "science, society or culture". This clause names all three domains up-front so the marker can score Task Response on the right rubric — without it, the essay reads as pure technology history and is capped on TR.

spans.text: At the heart of every modern computer is an array of tiny switches

highlight.label: Subject-fronted inversion
highlight.explanation: Place adverbial + inverted subject ("At the heart of X is Y") — a Band-7 grammar showcase. Replaces the broken "Computers consist of a full of small switches" while keeping the same image.

spans.text: whose two states map directly onto the 0 and 1 of Boolean algebra

highlight.label: Non-defining relative + precise term
highlight.explanation: "whose" introduces a non-defining relative — a more sophisticated reference than "which". Pairs it with the precise technical term "Boolean algebra" instead of vague "binary digits".

spans.text: — a correspondence
Claude Shannon formalised in his 1937 master's thesis

highlight.label: Named in-period figure + date
highlight.explanation: One specific, datable figure inside the chosen period — exactly what the feedback's Tier-1 PEEL guidance asks for. The original's only named figure (Morse, 1844) sits OUTSIDE the computer revolution.

spans.text: . By translating logical operations into electrical circuits, Shannon showed that any decision a machine needs to make could, in principle, be built from combinations of AND, OR and NOT gates.
Were a single processor to contain billions of such switches

highlight.label: Inverted Type-2 conditional
highlight.explanation: "Were S to V…" is an inverted second conditional — the highest-leverage Band-7 GRA showcase per character of effort. Replaces the childlike "If it has billions and trillions of switches, then it can go up to almost countless numbers".

spans.text: , the range of values it could represent would grow
astronomically, far beyond anything practical computation requires

highlight.label: Bounded intensifier (hedge)
highlight.explanation: "Astronomically, far beyond anything practical computation requires" — replaces the original's overreach ("any value, however large") with mathematically defensible hedging. Band 7 LR rewards precision, not exaggeration.

spans.text: . This insight turned abstract logic into practical engineering,
enabling reliable digital circuits that later powered scientific modelling, business automation and mass communication

highlight.label: Result clause naming impact
highlight.explanation: Present-participle result clause ("enabling…") names three concrete downstream impacts — science, business, communication. Bridges the mechanism in Body 1 to the broader influence the prompt actually asks about.

spans.text: What makes this binary substrate so powerful is

highlight.label: Pseudo-cleft (Band-7 showcase)
highlight.explanation: "What X is Y" is a pseudo-cleft — emphasises the mechanism and avoids the original's "The reason it matters is because" redundancy. Specifically called out in §6 of band7-grammar-showcases.

spans.text: the principle of encoding

highlight.label: Precise technical term
highlight.explanation: Names the third thesis mechanism explicitly. "Encoding" is the standard term in computer science and matches the precision Band 7 LR expects on technical topics.

spans.text: every character, image and instruction

highlight.label: Concrete lexical chain
highlight.explanation: Three concrete instances replace the original's listing ("every letter… every emoji… the graphic"). Parallel triplets are a Band-7 cohesion pattern — same grammatical shape, ascending generality.

spans.text: the machine handles ultimately reduces to a sequence of integers. Alan Turing's 1936 paper on computable numbers had already established that a single, general-purpose machine could execute any procedure expressible as a precise algorithm, and
von Neumann's stored-program architecture of 1945

highlight.label: Second named figure + date
highlight.explanation: Pairs Turing (1936) with von Neumann (1945) inside the same paragraph — gives Body 2 two anchored, datable references and demonstrates extended knowledge of the period.

spans.text: turned that idea into a usable design.
The ENIAC, completed in 1946 to compute artillery firing tables

highlight.label: Concrete worked example
highlight.explanation: Specific machine + specific date + specific use-case. PEEL Example move — replaces abstract claims with a real, in-period instantiation of the mathematics the bodies have argued for.

spans.text: , already embodied this principle: nearly eighteen thousand vacuum tubes were arranged, programmed and re-programmed entirely through the same handful of mathematical ideas Turing and von Neumann had set out a decade earlier. From then on, text, photographs, sound and even running programs were no longer distinct objects but
variations of one underlying form

highlight.label: Synthesising apposition
highlight.explanation: Restates the encoding claim in abstract terms — "variations of one underlying form" is the kind of figurative-but-precise phrasing that Band 7 LR rewards.

spans.text: — numbers stored in memory and processed by logic.
The cultural and scientific impact of these foundations was profound

highlight.label: Impact-paragraph topic sentence
highlight.explanation: Opens a dedicated paragraph on the science / society / culture dimensions the prompt explicitly asks about. Without this paragraph, the essay is technology history and the marker has nothing to score on the prompt's actual question.

spans.text: . In the sciences,
encoded computation allowed researchers to simulate phenomena — from weather systems to protein folding — that no purely analytical method could resolve

highlight.label: Scientific impact, named domains
highlight.explanation: Two concrete scientific domains (weather, protein folding) anchor the abstract claim about simulation. Replaces the original essay's drift into communication history.

spans.text: , and statistical analyses that once took months by hand could be completed in seconds. Society felt the same wave:
by the 1980s, banking, payroll and air-traffic control had all been re-implemented on digital substrates

highlight.label: Past-perfect passive triplet
highlight.explanation: "had all been re-implemented" — past perfect + passive packaged with three concrete societal domains. The kind of error-free complex sentence Band 7 GRA wants to see frequently.

spans.text: , while by the 2000s,
billions of ordinary conversations were being routed as packets of binary code

highlight.label: Past continuous passive
highlight.explanation: "were being routed" — past continuous passive carrying a culturally vivid claim. Pairs the band-7 grammar showcase with topic-precise vocabulary ("packets", "routed") drawn from the technology topic bank.

spans.text: . Even cultural memory shifted form, as
photographs, music and film, once anchored to physical media, were absorbed into the same numerical stream

highlight.label: Reduced relative + image-rich claim
highlight.explanation: "once anchored to physical media" — reduced relative clause functioning as a back-reference. The metaphor ("absorbed into the numerical stream") shows Band-7 figurative precision, which the original essay never attempts.

spans.text: fundamentally changing how people store, share and reinterpret the past

highlight.label: Result clause + parallel triplet
highlight.explanation: Present-participle result clause ("changing…") with three parallel verbs ("store, share, reinterpret"). Synthesises the cultural-impact case in one Band-7 closing move rather than restating it.

spans.text: Taken together, these examples show that

highlight.label: Synthesis-conclusion frame
highlight.explanation: The MISSING conclusion is the cheapest band uplift in the whole essay — without it, TR is capped at Band 5. "Taken together…" is a standard Band-7 conclusion frame that signals synthesis rather than mere restatement.

spans.text: mathematics did not merely assist the computer revolution; it supplied the very language

highlight.label: "Not merely X; Y" emphasis
highlight.explanation: Negative-emphatic correlative ("not merely…; it [also]…"). Promotes the central claim from informational to argumentative — exactly what a Band-7 conclusion sounds like.

spans.text: — binary, logical, encoded — in which computation became possible at all.
Without Shannon's algebra, Turing's machine and the practice of encoding

highlight.label: Parallel callback to bodies
highlight.explanation: Names the three pieces of evidence already developed (Shannon = Body 1, Turing = Body 2, encoding = thesis mechanism 3). Parallel triplet structure reinforces the synthesis without re-arguing it.

spans.text: , the twentieth century's most transformative technology —
and the scientific, economic and cultural change it carried with it

highlight.label: Three-domain callback
highlight.explanation: Repeats the science / society / culture triad from the intro and Body 3 in the final clause — closes the loop on the prompt's actual question and ensures TR is scored on the right rubric.

spans.text: — would have remained an idea on paper.
